Title: Course Welcome
Narration: Welcome to the California CNA Recertification Online Continuing Education Course. This 12-hour course is designed to help you strengthen your skills and knowledge as a certified nursing assistant. You will complete seven content modules covering infection control, resident rights, dementia care, mobility, nutrition, documentation, and a final review with exam. This course provides partial California CNA continuing education credit - online theory hours only. It does not complete your full renewal requirements. Estimated total time is 12 hours. You can pause and resume at any time, and your progress is saved automatically.

You can pause and resume at any time, and your progress is saved automatically.
Voice Notes: CNA = spell out C-N-A. California = Kal-ih-FORN-ya.

Title: How to Navigate the Course
Narration: To navigate this course, use the menu on the left side of your screen. Each module must be completed in order. You will need to view all lesson pages and pass each module knowledge check at 80 percent or higher before moving to the next module. Your active time in the course is tracked automatically. Do not open the course in multiple tabs at the same time, as this may affect your progress record. If you experience a technical problem, contact your course administrator before starting over.

Title: Compliance Disclaimers
Narration: Before you begin, please note three important reminders. First, this course provides partial California CNA continuing education credit - online theory hours only. It does not complete your full renewal requirements. Second, optional clinical support activities in this course are not California renewal clinical-hour credit. Third, your certificate will reflect online CE theory hours only - no clinical hours are included. If you have questions about your full renewal requirements, contact your employer or the California Department of Public Health.

Title: Chain of Infection
Narration: Infection spreads through a chain that has six links. The links are: the infectious agent - the pathogen that causes disease; the reservoir - where the pathogen lives; the portal of exit - how it leaves the reservoir; the mode of transmission - how it travels; the portal of entry - how it enters a new host; and the susceptible host - the person who becomes infected. Breaking any one of these links stops the infection from spreading. As a CNA, hand hygiene is your most powerful tool for breaking the chain.
Transcript: Infection spreads through a chain that has six links. The links are: the infectious agent - the pathogen that causes disease; the reservoir - where the pathogen lives; the portal of exit - how it leaves the reservoir; the mode of transmission - how it travels; the portal of entry - how it enters a new host; and the susceptible host - the person who becomes infected. Breaking any one of these links stops the infection from spreading. As a CNA, hand hygiene is your most powerful tool for breaking the chain.
Compliance Review Flag: TTS planning only; no audio production without authorization.

Title: Hand Hygiene
Narration: Hand hygiene is the single most effective measure for preventing infection. Wash your hands with soap and water for at least 20 seconds at the right moments: before and after resident contact, after removing gloves, after contact with body fluids, before and after eating, and after using the restroom. When hands are not visibly soiled, alcohol-based hand rub is acceptable. Rub all surfaces of the hands and fingers until completely dry - about 20 to 30 seconds. Never skip hand hygiene because you are busy.
Transcript: Hand hygiene is the single most effective measure for preventing infection. Wash your hands with soap and water for at least 20 seconds at the right moments: before and after resident contact, after removing gloves, after contact with body fluids, before and after eating, and after using the restroom. When hands are not visibly soiled, alcohol-based hand rub is acceptable. Rub all surfaces of the hands and fingers until completely dry - about 20 to 30 seconds. Never skip hand hygiene because you are busy.
Compliance Review Flag: TTS planning only; no audio production without authorization.

Title: Standard Precautions
Narration: Standard Precautions apply to every resident, every time - regardless of their diagnosis or how healthy they appear. Treat all blood, body fluids, non-intact skin, and mucous membranes as potentially infectious. Standard Precautions include: hand hygiene, use of appropriate personal protective equipment, safe sharps handling, and proper handling of soiled linen and equipment. They are not optional. They are the baseline for every care interaction.

They are the baseline for every care interaction.
Compliance Review Flag: TTS planning only; no audio production without authorization.

Title: PPE Donning and Doffing
Narration: Personal protective equipment - or PPE - must be put on and removed in the correct order to prevent contaminating yourself. To put on PPE, the order is: gown first, then mask or respirator, then eye protection or face shield, then gloves last. To remove PPE - which is called doffing - remove gloves first because they are the most contaminated. Then remove eye protection. Then remove the gown. Remove the mask last, handling only the ties or ear loops. Perform hand hygiene immediately after removing PPE.
Transcript: Personal protective equipment - or PPE - must be put on and removed in the correct order to prevent contaminating yourself. To remove PPE - which is called doffing - remove gloves first because they are the most contaminated. Then remove eye protection. Then remove the gown. Remove the mask last, handling only the ties or ear loops. Perform hand hygiene immediately after removing PPE.
Voice Notes: PPE = spell out P-P-E. Doffing = DOFF-ing.
Compliance Review Flag: TTS planning only; no audio production without authorization.

Title: Transmission-Based Precautions
Narration: When a resident has a known or suspected infectious condition, additional precautions are required beyond Standard Precautions. There are three types. Contact precautions are used for infections spread by direct or indirect contact with the resident or their environment - for example, MRSA or C. difficile. Droplet precautions are used for infections spread by respiratory droplets - for example, influenza. Airborne precautions are used for infections spread through smaller particles that remain in the air for longer periods - for example, active tuberculosis. Know which precautions apply to each resident on your assignment.

Droplet precautions are used for infections spread by respiratory droplets - for example, influenza. Airborne precautions are used for infections spread through smaller particles that remain in the air for longer periods - for example, active tuberculosis. Know which precautions apply to each resident on your assignment.
Voice Notes: MRSA = MER-suh. C. difficile = C. DIF-ih-seel. Tuberculosis = too-BER-kyoo-LOH-sis.

Title: Resident Rights Foundation
Narration: Every person living in a long-term care facility has legal rights protected by federal and state law. Key rights include: privacy - the right to personal space and confidentiality; dignity - the right to be treated with respect at all times; choice - the right to make decisions about care and daily life; informed consent - the right to understand and agree to treatments before they happen; freedom from restraints - the right to be free from physical or chemical restraints used for staff convenience; and the right to file grievances without fear of retaliation. As a CNA, you practice these rights in every interaction.
Transcript: Every person living in a long-term care facility has legal rights protected by federal and state law. Key rights include: privacy - the right to personal space and confidentiality; dignity - the right to be treated with respect at all times; choice - the right to make decisions about care and daily life; informed consent - the right to understand and agree to treatments before they happen; freedom from restraints - the right to be free from physical or chemical restraints used for staff convenience; and the right to file grievances without fear of retaliation. As a CNA, you practice these rights in every interaction.
Compliance Review Flag: TTS planning only; no audio production without authorization.

Title: Types of Abuse and Neglect
Narration: There are eight primary types of elder abuse recognized in California. Verbal abuse includes teasing, threats, and name-calling. Physical abuse includes hitting, kicking, shoving, or rough handling. Psychological abuse includes ridiculing, ignoring, or manipulating a resident. Involuntary seclusion means isolating a resident against their will. Abandonment means leaving a resident unattended or failing to plan for their care. Neglect is a pattern of failing to provide care. Sexual abuse includes any unwanted sexual contact or exposure. Financial abuse includes stealing money or using a resident's property without permission. Healthcare fraud - such as falsifying billing - is also recognized.
Transcript: There are eight primary types of elder abuse recognized in California. Verbal abuse includes teasing, threats, and name-calling. Physical abuse includes hitting, kicking, shoving, or rough handling. Psychological abuse includes ridiculing, ignoring, or manipulating a resident. Involuntary seclusion means isolating a resident against their will. Abandonment means leaving a resident unattended or failing to plan for their care. Neglect is a pattern of failing to provide care. Sexual abuse includes any unwanted sexual contact or exposure. Financial abuse includes stealing money or using a resident's property without permission. Healthcare fraud - such as falsifying billing - is also recognized.
Compliance Review Flag: TTS planning only; no audio production without authorization.

Title: Signs and Symptoms of Abuse
Narration: You may not witness abuse directly. Look for these warning signs. Behavioral signs include new depression, confusion, withdrawal, flinching at touch, and being overly anxious to please a caregiver. Physical signs include unexplained bruises, burns, or scars, unkempt appearance, and preventable pressure injuries. Environmental signs include unsafe or unsanitary living conditions and not being allowed private conversations with visitors. When you observe any of these signs, document what you see objectively and report immediately to the licensed nurse.
Transcript: You may not witness abuse directly. Look for these warning signs. Behavioral signs include new depression, confusion, withdrawal, flinching at touch, and being overly anxious to please a caregiver. Physical signs include unexplained bruises, burns, or scars, unkempt appearance, and preventable pressure injuries. Environmental signs include unsafe or unsanitary living conditions and not being allowed private conversations with visitors. When you observe any of these signs, document what you see objectively and report immediately to the licensed nurse.
Compliance Review Flag: TTS planning only; no audio production without authorization.

Title: Mandated Reporter Obligations
Narration: In California, all CNAs are mandated reporters. This is a legal obligation - not a choice. You must report known or suspected abuse immediately. You do not need to prove that abuse occurred. Reasonable suspicion is enough. Report first to the licensed nurse, then follow your facility's policy for formal reporting. If your supervisor does not act, you may report directly to Adult Protective Services or the Long-Term Care Ombudsman. Failure to report is a crime. Reports made in good faith are legally protected.
Transcript: In California, all CNAs are mandated reporters. This is a legal obligation - not a choice. You must report known or suspected abuse immediately. You do not need to prove that abuse occurred. Reasonable suspicion is enough. Report first to the licensed nurse, then follow your facility's policy for formal reporting. If your supervisor does not act, you may report directly to Adult Protective Services or the Long-Term Care Ombudsman. Failure to report is a crime. Reports made in good faith are legally protected.
Compliance Review Flag: TTS planning only; no audio production without authorization.

Title: Prevention and Professional Boundaries
Narration: You can help prevent abuse by recognizing your own stress triggers and asking for help when you need it. Answer call lights promptly. Follow your facility's policies consistently. Encourage family visits - isolated residents are more vulnerable. Speak up if you see a coworker losing patience. Professional boundaries protect both you and the residents you care for. Do not accept gifts, share personal financial problems with residents, or develop romantic relationships with residents. Boundary violations are a form of abuse - even if the resident initiates the contact.
Transcript: You can help prevent abuse by recognizing your own stress triggers and asking for help when you need it. Answer call lights promptly. Follow your facility's policies consistently. Do not accept gifts, share personal financial problems with residents, or develop romantic relationships with residents. Boundary violations are a form of abuse - even if the resident initiates the contact.

Title: Understanding Dementia
Narration: Dementia is not a single disease - it is a group of symptoms affecting memory, thinking, and daily function. Alzheimer's disease is the most common cause, accounting for 60 to 80 percent of cases. Dementia is not a normal part of aging. It is progressive - symptoms worsen over time - and currently there is no cure. An important distinction: delirium is sudden confusion caused by infection, medication change, or dehydration, and is often reversible. Dementia develops gradually and is not reversible. If a resident who has been stable suddenly becomes confused, report this to the licensed nurse immediately - it may be delirium, not worsening dementia.
Transcript: Dementia is not a single disease - it is a group of symptoms affecting memory, thinking, and daily function. Title: Communication Strategies
Narration: Effective communication with residents who have dementia requires adjusting your approach. Approach from the front and make eye contact before speaking. Use the resident's name. Speak slowly and clearly in short sentences - one idea at a time. Use a calm, warm tone. Allow extra time for responses. Give one-step directions. Use gentle touch, pointing, and gestures to support understanding. Do not argue or correct - instead, validate the emotion behind what the resident is saying. For example: 'You miss your family. Tell me about them.' Use reminiscence to connect through past experiences.

Title: Cultural Sensitivity and Spiritual Respect
Narration: Cultural sensitivity means being aware of and respectful toward cultural differences in beliefs, values, and practices. Do not assume - ask residents and families about their preferences. Respect dietary practices such as halal, kosher, or vegetarian requirements. Respect modesty preferences including requests for same-gender caregivers. Respect spiritual practices - protect time for prayer, handle religious objects with care, and facilitate visits from spiritual leaders. Use professional interpreter services for medical communication - do not use family members, especially children, as interpreters. At end of life, ask about cultural and religious practices - do not assume.
Transcript: Cultural sensitivity means being aware of and respectful toward cultural differences in beliefs, values, and practices. Do not assume - ask residents and families about their preferences. Title: End-of-Life Care and Grief
Narration: Elisabeth Kubler-Ross identified five stages of grief: denial, anger, bargaining, depression, and acceptance. Not everyone goes through all stages, and they may not occur in order. Your role is not to push someone through the stages - it is to be present, listen, and provide comfort. Signs that death may be approaching include decreased appetite, mottling of the skin, Cheyne-Stokes breathing, cool extremities, and decreased responsiveness. Keep the resident clean and comfortable. Provide mouth care. Speak softly - hearing may be the last sense to go. Allow family to be present and offer your support.

Title: Trauma-Informed Care
Narration: Trauma-informed care means recognizing that many residents have experienced trauma - abuse, war, loss, or medical trauma - and that this affects how they respond to care. The core principles are safety, trustworthiness, choice, collaboration, and empowerment. In practice: announce yourself before entering, explain each step of care before you do it, offer small choices, work with the resident rather than doing things to them, and recognize their strengths. If a resident becomes agitated, stay calm, lower your voice, give space, and acknowledge the feeling: 'I can see you are upset. I want to help.'

Title: Body Mechanics
Narration: Body mechanics is the use of correct muscle groups and body positions to prevent injury during physical activity. The core principles are: maintain a wide base of support with feet shoulder-width apart. Bend at the knees and hips - not the waist. Keep the load close to your body. Tighten your core muscles before lifting. Pivot your feet to change direction - never twist. Push rather than pull when possible. Know your limits - if a transfer or load requires more than one person, get help or use a mechanical lift. Back injuries are the number one workplace injury for CNAs.

Title: Safe Transfers and Ambulation
Narration: Before any transfer, check the care plan for the resident's weight-bearing status, required assistance level, and equipment needed. Explain what you are doing to the resident. Lock all wheels on the bed and wheelchair. Apply the gait belt snugly over clothing at the waist. Clear the path of obstacles. Position the wheelchair at a 45-degree angle on the resident's stronger side. Count together before moving. Guide the resident's movement - do not lift them. If the care plan requires two-person assist, do not attempt the transfer alone. When assisting with ambulation, stand slightly behind and to the side of the resident's weaker side.
Transcript: Before any transfer, check the care plan for the resident's weight-bearing status, required assistance level, and equipment needed. Explain what you are doing to the resident. Lock all wheels on the bed and wheelchair. Apply the gait belt snugly over clothing at the waist. Clear the path of obstacles. Position the wheelchair at a 45-degree angle on the resident's stronger side. Count together before moving. Guide the resident's movement - do not lift them. If the care plan requires two-person assist, do not attempt the transfer alone. When assisting with ambulation, stand slightly behind and to the side of the resident's weaker side.

Lesson Title: Range of Motion and Restorative Care
Title: Range of Motion and Restorative Care
Narration: Range of motion refers to the full extent to which a joint can move. When residents are immobile, joints can become stiff - a condition called contracture. Active ROM means the resident performs the movements independently. Active-assistive ROM means the resident has some help from you. Passive ROM means you move the joints for the resident. Always support the limb above and below the joint. Move gently - never force a joint past the point of resistance or pain. In restorative care, encourage residents to do as much as they safely can for themselves. Support independence - do not take over.
Transcript: Range of motion refers to the full extent to which a joint can move. When residents are immobile, joints can become stiff - a condition called contracture. Active ROM means the resident performs the movements independently. Active-assistive ROM means the resident has some help from you. Passive ROM means you move the joints for the resident. Always support the limb above and below the joint. Move gently - never force a joint past the point of resistance or pain. In restorative care, encourage residents to do as much as they safely can for themselves. Support independence - do not take over.
Voice Notes: Contracture = con-TRACK-chur. Proximal = PROK-sih-mul. Distal = DIS-tul.
Compliance Review Flag: TTS planning only; no audio production without authorization.

Title: Fall Prevention
Narration: Falls are the leading cause of injury in long-term care. Your CNA responsibilities for fall prevention include: answering call lights promptly - many falls happen when residents try to get up alone because help did not come. Lock all wheels before every transfer. Ensure residents wear non-skid footwear. Keep pathways clear and clean up spills immediately. Keep the bed in the lowest position. Keep essentials - call light, water, glasses - within the resident's reach. Use bed alarms as directed by the care plan. Assist with scheduled toileting. Report near-falls - a near-fall today may be a fall tomorrow.

Title: Emergency Procedures
Narration: For fire emergencies, remember RACE: Rescue residents in immediate danger. Alarm - pull the fire alarm and call 911. Contain the fire by closing doors and windows. Extinguish a small fire if safe - or Evacuate if the fire is spreading. To use a fire extinguisher, remember PASS: Pull the pin. Aim at the base of the fire - not the flames. Squeeze the handle. Sweep from side to side. For a choking emergency: ask if the resident can speak or cough. If not, stand behind them, place your fist above the navel, grasp it with both hands, and deliver firm upward abdominal thrusts until the obstruction is cleared.
Transcript: For fire emergencies, remember RACE: Rescue residents in immediate danger. Alarm - pull the fire alarm and call 911. Contain the fire by closing doors and windows. Sweep from side to side. For a choking emergency: ask if the resident can speak or cough. If not, stand behind them, place your fist above the navel, grasp it with both hands, and deliver firm upward abdominal thrusts until the obstruction is cleared.

Module Title: Nutrition, Skin Integrity, and Vital Signs
Title: Nutrition and Hydration
Narration: The body needs six basic nutrients: carbohydrates for energy, proteins to build and repair tissues, fats for energy and vitamin absorption, vitamins to support body processes, minerals for bone health and fluid balance, and water - the most essential nutrient of all. Older adults are at high risk for dehydration because the sensation of thirst decreases with age, kidneys become less efficient, some medications increase fluid loss, and residents with cognitive impairment may not ask for fluids. Signs of dehydration include dry mouth, dark urine, confusion, dizziness, and dry skin. Offer fluids regularly - do not wait for the resident to ask.

Module Title: Nutrition, Skin Integrity, and Vital Signs
Title: Feeding Assistance and Aspiration Prevention
Narration: When providing feeding assistance, position the resident upright at 60 to 90 degrees. Sit at their eye level - do not stand over them. Identify each food. Offer small bites - about half a teaspoon. Allow full swallowing time between bites. Offer fluids frequently. Never rush a meal. Watch for warning signs of aspiration: coughing, gagging, color changes, or a wet or gurgling voice after swallowing. If you observe any of these signs, stop feeding immediately, keep the resident upright, call for help, and report to the licensed nurse. If thickened liquids are ordered, use the ordered consistency for all liquids - every time.

Module Title: Nutrition, Skin Integrity, and Vital Signs
Lesson Title: Skin Integrity and Pressure Injury Prevention
Title: Skin Integrity and Pressure Injury Prevention
Narration: A pressure injury is damage to the skin and underlying tissue caused by prolonged pressure over a bony prominence. The most common site is the sacrum - the tailbone. Risk factors include immobility, incontinence, poor nutrition, dehydration, advanced age, and friction from improper repositioning. To prevent pressure injuries: reposition at least every two hours. Keep skin clean and dry. Change soiled linens and briefs promptly. Use pressure-relieving devices as ordered. Inspect skin during every care episode. Lift - do not drag - during repositioning. Report any redness, warmth, or skin breakdown immediately to the licensed nurse.

Report any redness, warmth, or skin breakdown immediately to the licensed nurse.
Voice Notes: Sacrum = SAY-krum. Bony prominence = BOH-nee PROM-ih-nense.

Module Title: Nutrition, Skin Integrity, and Vital Signs
Title: Vital Signs
Narration: The six vital signs you will monitor are temperature, pulse, respirations, blood pressure, oxygen saturation, and pain. Normal ranges are: temperature 97 to 99 degrees Fahrenheit for oral measurement; pulse 60 to 100 beats per minute; respirations 12 to 20 breaths per minute; blood pressure systolic 90 to 120, diastolic 60 to 80; oxygen saturation 95 to 100 percent; and pain rated 0 to 10. Report any reading significantly outside these ranges or different from the resident's baseline immediately. An oxygen saturation below 90 percent is a medical emergency - report it right away.

Normal ranges are: temperature 97 to 99 degrees Fahrenheit for oral measurement; pulse 60 to 100 beats per minute; respirations 12 to 20 breaths per minute; blood pressure systolic 90 to 120, diastolic 60 to 80; oxygen saturation 95 to 100 percent; and pain rated 0 to 10. Report any reading significantly outside these ranges or different from the resident's baseline immediately. An oxygen saturation below 90 percent is a medical emergency - report it right away.
Voice Notes: Systolic = sis-TOL-ick. Diastolic = dy-uh-STOL-ick. SpO2 = S-P-O-two.

Module Title: Nutrition, Skin Integrity, and Vital Signs
Title: Monitoring, Documentation, and Reporting
Narration: When intake and output monitoring is ordered, measure and record all fluids taken in and all fluids leaving the body. Use a graduated container for urine output - do not estimate. Common conversions: one cup equals 240 milliliters; one carton of milk equals 240 milliliters. For weight monitoring, weigh the resident at the same time of day on the same scale in similar clothing. Report any weight loss of 5 percent or more in 30 days, or any sudden weight gain. Both can indicate a significant change in the resident's condition.
Transcript: When intake and output monitoring is ordered, measure and record all fluids taken in and all fluids leaving the body. Use a graduated container for urine output - do not estimate. Common conversions: one cup equals 240 milliliters; one carton of milk equals 240 milliliters. For weight monitoring, weigh the resident at the same time of day on the same scale in similar clothing. Report any weight loss of 5 percent or more in 30 days, or any sudden weight gain. Both can indicate a significant change in the resident's condition.

Module Title: Documentation, Change of Condition, and Scope of Practice
Title: Objective vs. Subjective Observations
Narration: As a CNA, you are the most important observer on the healthcare team because you spend the most time with residents. Observations fall into two categories. Objective observations are what you can see, measure, hear, feel, or smell - for example, a bruise 3 centimeters in diameter on the left forearm, or a blood pressure of 150 over 95. Subjective observations are what the resident tells you - for example, 'I feel dizzy' or 'My stomach hurts.' Document and report both. Always use the resident's exact words in quotation marks for subjective observations.

Subjective observations are what the resident tells you - for example, 'I feel dizzy' or 'My stomach hurts.' Document and report both. Always use the resident's exact words in quotation marks for subjective observations.

Module Title: Documentation, Change of Condition, and Scope of Practice
Title: Documentation Standards
Narration: Your documentation is a legal record. Follow these rules every time. Document promptly - as soon as possible after the event. Be objective - write what happened, not what you think happened. Be specific - instead of 'ate well,' write 'consumed 75 percent of lunch.' Use the resident's exact words in quotation marks. Use only approved abbreviations. Never use white-out or erase - draw a single line through errors, write the word error, initial, and date. Sign every entry with your name, CNA title, date, and time. Never chart in advance. Never chart for someone else. If it is not documented, legally it did not happen.

Compliance Review Flag: Uses fictional/de-identified examples only.

Module Title: Documentation, Change of Condition, and Scope of Practice
Title: Change of Condition Reporting
Narration: A change of condition is any significant difference in a resident's status from their normal baseline. Report immediately to the licensed nurse if you observe new confusion, vital sign changes, new or worsening pain, skin changes, falls, breathing difficulty, changes in intake or output, decreased mobility, or emotional changes. Use the SBAR framework: Situation - who you are calling about and why. Background - the resident's relevant history. Assessment - what you observed. Recommendation - what you believe is needed. Be specific. Use numbers and measurements. Include the time you noticed the change. When in doubt - report.

Be specific. Use numbers and measurements. Include the time you noticed the change. When in doubt - report.

Module Title: Documentation, Change of Condition, and Scope of Practice
Title: CNA Scope of Practice
Narration: Your scope of practice defines what you are legally allowed to do as a CNA. CNAs can assist with activities of daily living, measure vital signs, perform range of motion exercises, assist with transfers and ambulation, observe and document, and provide comfort measures. CNAs cannot administer medications - including over-the-counter products - perform sterile procedures, diagnose conditions, give medical advice, take physician orders, or modify the care plan. If someone asks you to perform a task outside your scope, decline clearly, explain your scope, and report the request to the charge nurse. When in doubt, ask the licensed nurse before acting.

Module Title: Documentation, Change of Condition, and Scope of Practice
Title: Professionalism, Ethics, and Delegation
Narration: Professionalism as a CNA means being reliable, maintaining your appearance, keeping a positive and respectful attitude, maintaining confidentiality - including on social media - owning your mistakes, working as a team, and continuing to learn. Delegation flows from the licensed nurse to you - not from CNA to CNA. When you receive a delegated task, understand the instructions, perform the task as directed, and report back when done. Never accept a task you are not trained to perform - it is your right and responsibility to say no. Your professional behavior protects your residents and your certification.

Lesson Title: Combined Review and Final Exam Introduction
Title: Combined Review and Final Exam Introduction
Narration: You have completed all six content modules. This final module includes a brief review of key concepts, your cumulative final exam, and the required affidavit of completion. The final exam contains 25 questions drawn from a pool of 50. You need to answer 20 questions correctly - that is 80 percent - to pass. You have two attempts and 30 minutes per attempt. After passing the exam, you will complete a brief affidavit attesting that you personally completed all course activities. Your certificate will be released once all required gates are satisfied.

Title: Certificate and Next Steps
Narration: Your certificate of completion will reflect 12 hours of online continuing education theory. It will not include clinical hours or optional support activities. Keep a copy of your certificate and submit it as required by your employer or the California Department of Public Health as part of your CNA renewal application. This course is partial credit only - verify your full renewal requirements with CDPH or your employer. Thank you for completing this course. The care and dedication you bring to your residents makes a real difference.
